ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai basil crispy chicken rice. FOODON_00002809 (chicken dish) used as the closest broader class anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Thai kra pao crispy chicken rice. A0CKM (chicken meat dish, mixed preparation) used as the closest defensible analogous anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Thai rice dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of kra pao basil crispy chicken rice weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a serving of deep-fried chicken pieces in Thai basil sauce over a portion of steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: R4 Kra-Pao Basil Crispy Chicken Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010410). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable deep-fried chicken and rice dishes (USDA SR Legacy #05062 and #20444 composite); value set to 1.2 g/100g as a conservative estimate. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported by HPB and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (steamed, Asian-style) analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kra pao basil crispy chicken rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 72 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white jasmine rice, which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care). The chicken, basil sauce, and fat components do not materially alter the GI of the rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 52.9) / 100 ≈ 38.1 for a 350 g plate (carbohydrate 15.12 g/100g × 3.5 = 52.9 g); however, noting that the rice fraction is approximately 150 g of the 350 g plate (carbohydrate ~37.5 g from rice), GL is reported conservatively at 19.1 based on the rice portion carbohydrate contribution of 26.5 g: (72 × 26.5) / 100 = 19.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from multi-condiment sauce base (fish sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce) at 324 mg/100g with insufficient potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, ask for sauce on the side, and avoid additional chilli sauce, as sodium at 324 mg/100g across a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,134 mg sodium — nearly half the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base raises glycaemic load; added sugars in oyster and fish sauce contribute to postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute brown rice or reduce the rice portion to lower glycaemic load, request reduced sauce to minimise added sugar, and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response from the high-GI white rice base (GI ≈ 72).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation increases saturated fat and AGE content; sodium load is elevated; cholesterol at 13 mg/100g is moderate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption due to the deep-fried preparation contributing saturated fat and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), combined with a sodium load of 324 mg/100g that increases cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs that directly worsen joint inflammation; nightshade ingredient (chilli) may exacerbate sensitivity in susceptible individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its standard deep-fried form, as the crispy chicken preparation generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; requesting a stir-fried preparation without deep-frying is the recommended modification.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kra Pao Basil Crispy Chicken Rice (Thai: ข้าวกะเพราไก่กรอบ; romanized: Khao Kra Pao Kai Krob) is a Thai rice dish consisting of deep-fried crispy chicken pieces tossed in a fragrant stir-fried sauce made with Thai holy basil (kra pao / Ocimum tenuiflorum), garlic, fresh chilli, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, served over steamed white jasmine rice. The dish is a popular adaptation of the classic Thai street-food dish pad kra pao (stir-fried holy basil), elevated with a deep-fried chicken preparation that delivers a contrasting crunchy texture against the aromatic, slightly spicy, and umami-rich basil sauce. Per 100 g, the dish provides 116 kcal, 4.96 g protein, 4.0 g fat, 15.12 g carbohydrate, 1.56 g sugar, 1.0 g dietary fibre, and 324 mg sodium. The sodium content is clinically significant, driven by the combination of fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce. The white rice base carries a high glycaemic index (GI ≈ 72), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which is a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or cardiovascular disease. Thai holy basil contributes polyphenols and antioxidants but does not offset the clinical concerns from the cooking method and condiment sodium load. This dish is commonly served as a lunch or dinner main course in Thai restaurants and hawker-style food courts across Singapore and Thailand.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010410, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore-served dishes. The dish identity is exact. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy comparable entries and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white jasmine rice, which is the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, and is clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable deep-fried chicken and steamed white rice composites, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Thai basil crispy chicken rice preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white jasmine rice (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; fish sauce and oyster sauce brands used in preparation may vary. 5. The 'R4' prefix in the original input name is a restaurant menu code and has been removed from the canonical food_name to avoid menu-style clutter, but is preserved in the aliases array.
